concise:
Ayah 1–10 · linked video: 01. Al-Muzzammil (Ayah 1-10)

Surah Al-Muzzammil: Commentary on Ayahs 1-10

Ayah 1

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمُزَّمِّلُ

O you wrapped in garments

Surah Al-Muzzammil is one of the most beautiful chapters of the Quran, describing the balance between the inner spiritual life and the outer calling of the believer through the character of the Prophet ﷺ. It begins with one of the beloved names given to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ: Al-Muzzammil.

The word Muzzammil comes from the verb zammala, meaning to wrap oneself up in clothes or a blanket. Originally Mutazammil, the word transforms through Arabic morphology as the letters merge together. This term captures the Prophet ﷺ in a particular state—wrapped in his garment, perhaps resting at night.

Allah ﷻ uses this as an affectionate nickname for His Messenger ﷺ, “O you who is wrapped in the blanket.” This address contains layers of meaning. On one level, it suggests comfort and rest—someone wrapped comfortably in their covering. Yet the act of wrapping oneself can also indicate vulnerability, worry, or seeking protection from fear or cold. Both dimensions are captured in this moment: the Prophet ﷺ is in a state of both relaxation and concern, comfort and apprehension. Allah ﷻ addresses him in this intimate state in the middle of the night.

Ayah 2

قُمِ ٱلَّيْلَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا

Stand [in prayer during] the night, except for a little

After calling him by this tender name, Allah ﷻ gives a direct command: قُمِ ٱلَّيْلَ —stand the night. The instruction is to stand in prayer during most of the night, إِلَّا قَلِيلًا—except for a little portion. This is the establishment of Qiyam al-Layl, the night prayer.

It’s essential to understand that this was not optional for the Prophet ﷺ. While for us, Qiyam al-Layl is voluntary worship, something extra and beloved but not obligatory, for the Messenger of Allah ﷺ it was mandatory. This night vigil was ordained specifically for him as preparation for the immense responsibility he was about to carry.

Ayah 3

نِّصْفَهُۥٓ أَوِ ٱنقُصْ مِنْهُ قَلِيلًا

Half of it—or subtract from it a little

Allah ﷻ provides flexibility within the command: stand at least half the night in prayer, or you may reduce it slightly from half. The instruction is clear but merciful—do at least half, and if needed, a bit less than that is acceptable.

Ayah 4

أَوْ زِدْ عَلَيْهِ وَرَتِّلِ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ تَرْتِيلًا

Or add to it, and recite the Quran with measured recitation

The verse continues: or increase upon it, going beyond half the night if you’re able. Then comes a critical instruction: وَرَتِّلِ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ تَرْتِيلًا —recite the Quran in slow, rhythmic, clear, deliberate fashion.

Tarteel means to slow down and articulate every syllable with precision, to give every letter its due right. This is the spirit of night prayer—you have extra time, the day’s activities are done, there are no distractions. Therefore, when you recite the Quran, recite it with exceptional care and slowness.

The irony of this command in our time is striking. This concept of tarteel became institutionalized in Ramadan through Taraweeh prayers. Umar (RA) facilitated this practice for the community, condensing what was an all-night vigil into a manageable hour or two so working Muslims could participate. Yet the spirit of it was supposed to remain—extra time for slow, contemplative recitation of Allah’s words.

Instead, what has happened? We have imams racing through the Quran. Masjids compete on speed: “This one finishes in 25 minutes, that one takes 45 minutes.” We actually have speed records for Taraweeh. People boast about how quickly they can complete twenty rak’ahs or finish an entire juz. Children are rushed through the 29th juz on the 26th night because somehow everyone decided the Quran must be completed by the 27th night, though no such requirement exists.

The command was وَرَتِّلِ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ تَرْتِيلًا—slow it down! Even if we don’t finish the entire Quran in Ramadan, even if we only pray however many rak’ahs we pray, the instruction is clear: recite properly. Don’t make a mockery of it. When recitation is rushed, even those who understand Arabic cannot comprehend what’s being said. Even those who have memorized the Quran lose track.

This is not the Quran’s instruction. The Quran went out of its way to specify that in this particular prayer, you must slow down your recitation. Yes, you can recite at a normal pace in other prayers, but in Qiyam, Allah ﷻ emphasized: slow it down. Yet when an imam with a beautiful voice recites slowly and deliberately, people complain: “Can you speed it up a little?”

Consider the context of this revelation. This is one of the earliest surahs revealed, within the first four to seven revelations. The Prophet ﷺ is being told to stand for half the night, a third of the night—at least two to three hours minimum. How much Quran had been revealed at this point? Not even half a juz. Perhaps Surah Al-Fatihah completely, some passages here and there, some ayahs from other early surahs. That’s it.

He’s going to stand for half the night reciting what? The same passages, over and over again. He would recite them, then return and recite them again, because the point isn’t just to get through the material. The point is for the Quran to enter your soul, to run through your blood, to penetrate your entire being. It’s not merely something you know intellectually—it must become part of who you are at your core.

Ayah 5

إِنَّا سَنُلْقِى عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا

Indeed, We will cast upon you a heavy word

Why does the Quran need to penetrate so deeply? Why must the Prophet ﷺ be trained like this in the middle of the night? Allah ﷻ explains: إِنَّا سَنُلْقِى عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا—We are about to throw upon you, to bring you into contact with, a word that is extremely heavy.

قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا—a heavy word, a weighty statement. What makes it heavy? The responsibility that comes with it. The responsibility is about to be given to you to take this message public, to deliver it to the world, and it will not be easy.

To gain the strength necessary for this mission, you must draw closer to Allah ﷻ. The closer you are to Him, the stronger you will become. And you draw close to Allah ﷻ when you recite His words calmly, deliberately, in the stillness of the night.

Ayah 6

إِنَّ نَاشِئَةَ ٱلَّيْلِ هِىَ أَشَدُّ وَطْـًٔا وَأَقْوَمُ قِيلًا

Indeed, the hours of the night are more effective for concurrence [of heart and tongue] and more suitable for words

Now Allah ﷻ explains the benefits of night prayer specifically. إِنَّ نَاشِئَةَ ٱلَّيْلِ—no doubt about it, this getting up in the night, this worship performed in the night hours—هِىَ أَشَدُّ وَطْـًٔا—is more firm when it comes to planting your feet.

The word wat’ refers to stepping firmly on the ground, leaving an imprint, planting yourself solidly. Why is night worship more firm? Because there are no distractions. Nobody else is calling you. There are no other obligations. The world is asleep. Your feet are planted more firmly in worship.

This worship you perform at that time will plant you deeper, make you more firmly established. The imagery here is profound—when the Prophet’s ﷺ feet are planted firmly on the ground in prayer, his feet will be planted firmly when he stands to deliver his message. There’s a connection in the Quran between being firmly planted in prayer and being firmly planted in conviction and mission.

وَأَقْوَمُ قِيلًا—and it is more upright in terms of speech. Your words during this time are not filled with the distraction of anything else. This is the best time to speak, the most conducive time for communication with Allah ﷻ. Some scholars note that in the last portion of the night, Allah ﷻ descends to the lowest heaven, making it the closest, most intimate time for conversation with Him.

Ayah 7

إِنَّ لَكَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ سَبْحًا طَوِيلًا

Indeed, for you by day is prolonged occupation

إِنَّ لَكَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ سَبْحًا طَوِيلًا—no doubt, for you in the daytime there is extensive, prolonged activity.

The word سَبْحًا literally comes from sibahah, swimming. When you’re swimming, can you just stop? No, you must keep moving. It’s relentless activity. Even if you stay in one place, you must keep paddling to stay afloat. That’s the nature of your work during the day—it will be long and relentless, full of continuous demands.

Ayah 8

وَٱذْكُرِ ٱسْمَ رَبِّكَ وَتَبَتَّلْ إِلَيْهِ تَبْتِيلًا

And remember the name of your Lord and devote yourself to Him with [complete] devotion

وَٱذْكُرِ ٱسْمَ رَبِّكَ—make mention of the name of your Master. Remember Allah ﷻ, invoke His beautiful names: Ar-Rahman, Ar-Raheem.

وَتَبَتَّلْ إِلَيْهِ تَبْتِيلًا—and cut yourself off toward Him completely. The root word batala means to cut off, to separate. Normally we would expect the phrase to be either تَبَتَّلْ إِلَيْهِ (cut yourself off to Him) or تَبَتَّلْ عَنْهُ (cut yourself off from others). But the Quran combines both meanings in this unique construction.

Disconnect yourself from everything else and connect exclusively to Allah ﷻ. But there’s more—by doing this, you will help others disconnect from distractions and connect to Allah ﷻ. You will set the example. This isn’t just for you. When you become the model of devotion, everyone who follows you, the billions who will come after you and want to connect with Allah ﷻ, will now have a legacy to follow.

If this instruction were only for the Prophet’s ﷺ personal benefit, the phrasing would be different. But because it impacts both him and those who follow him, this unique construction captures both dimensions.

Notice the progression: first the Prophet ﷺ is told to cut himself off from society, to devote his nights to Allah ﷻ. Earlier, he was reminded that his days will be full of relentless activity. There’s a critical connection between these two realities, and we see this connection develop further in Surah Al-Muzzammil’s sister surah, the next chapter.

Here I want to share the spiritual foundations of da’wah, of calling people to Allah ﷻ. The golden rule that you and I must constantly remind ourselves of is this: whatever comes from the heart enters the heart. Not speeches, not talk, not mere knowledge—but when someone feels something to the core of their being, and that conviction emerges through the sincerity with which they share it with others, that sincerity itself has an impact.

People don’t just hear words. They sense genuineness. They can tell when someone truly believes what they’re saying, and that recognition touches them. The Prophet ﷺ didn’t just teach truth—he carried an aura of it. There was something about him. When he spoke, you could tell he meant every word.

You can sense when someone gives a speech and they’re absolutely convinced of what they’re saying, versus when it’s theatrics, hollow and empty. Even with identical content, you can tell the difference. We have an innate ability to recognize when something comes from the heart and when it doesn’t. Those with insight can especially tell: this is performance, or this is genuine advice being given because someone truly cares about me.

For your message to come from your heart, it must first enter your heart. That’s what the night is for. The night is when the message penetrates your heart. During the day, you deliver it to others, but at night, you receive it yourself.

Ayah 9

رَّبُّ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَٱتَّخِذْهُ وَكِيلًا

[He is] the Lord of the East and the West; there is no deity except Him, so take Him as Disposer of [your] affairs

رَّبُّ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ—He is the Master of the entire East and the entire West. لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ—there is no one worthy of worship and obedience in any way, shape or form except Him.

فَٱتَّخِذْهُ وَكِيلًا—then take Him as your Wakeel, as the One to handle all your affairs. The word wakeel refers to someone you entrust with your matters, who disposes of your affairs on your behalf. Leave your issues in Allah’s hands. Rely on Him completely. Let Him be the One you depend on.

Ayah 10

وَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَٱهْجُرْهُمْ هَجْرًا جَمِيلًا

And be patient over what they say and avoid them with gracious avoidance

وَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ—and be patient over what they are saying. Now Allah ﷻ tells him: be patient with their words, with what they say about you.

This introduces a profound concept that appears throughout the Quran. The Prophet ﷺ has two types of conversations. He has conversations with Allah ﷻ, and he has conversations with people. The conversation with Allah ﷻ is beautiful, empowering, rejuvenating. But the conversation with people is often ugly, offensive, insulting, hurtful.

He must engage in both conversations. But one conversation prepares him for the other. When you talk to people and it becomes depressing, when you run out of steam and wonder, “Why would I want to do this? Why would I talk to someone who insults me? They already insulted me yesterday. They already mocked me and my family. They already questioned my integrity as a human being. Why would I put myself in that position again?”

Allah ﷻ teaches His Messenger ﷺ: the only way you will be able to set your dignity aside—because this mission is more important than your personal dignity—is if you go back to Allah ﷻ and humble yourself before Him late at night. Then you will remember that no matter how much people put you down, that doesn’t take your true honor away. Your honor comes from Me. I have given you honor.

This is easy to talk about but incredibly difficult to apply. When you talk to someone about the deen and they insult you, your natural reaction is: “I’m not talking to that person again. Forget them. Who do they think they are, talking to me like that?”

Now imagine—who are we compared to the Messenger ﷺ? If there was ever a human being in a position to say, “Who do you think you’re talking to?”—if there was ever anyone who had that right, it was the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Do you realize what I’m sharing with you? Do you realize where this comes from? Do you understand the connections I have? I am the last messenger to humanity. Do you comprehend the consequences of rejecting me?

Yet when he heard insults, he couldn’t simply turn away. And I must add to this discussion because it’s critical to understand: there are different types of people with different levels of sensitivity. Some people are so coarse, so foul-mouthed and corrupt, that they’re accustomed to filth. They hear filth constantly, they speak filth, they’re surrounded by it. When you curse such a person, what happens? Nothing. They respond in kind, and it escalates.

But then you have decent, respectful people with proper upbringing. They go to their schools, their workplaces, their social gatherings, and they treat others with respect and expect to be treated with respect in return. When you enter your workplace, you don’t mentally prepare to hear curses or obscenities. You don’t expect someone to mock you or say something degrading. You simply don’t expect that.

The Prophet ﷺ was the most dignified, the most honorable human being. He carried himself with utmost respect and nobility. For someone of his stature to be subjected to insults, mockery and abuse—this required extraordinary patience. And the source of that patience? The nights spent in intimate conversation with Allah ﷻ, where he was reminded of his true worth and given the strength to continue his mission despite the ugliness he faced during the day.

This is the balance Al-Muzzammil establishes: the inner spiritual life that fortifies you for the outer challenges of calling people to truth.

Ayah 10–20 · linked video: 02. Al-Muzzammil (Ayah 10-20)

Surah Al-Muzzammil: Ayahs 10-20

Ayah 10

وَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَٱهْجُرْهُمْ هَجْرًا جَمِيلًا

And be patient over what they say, and leave them with gracious abandonment.

To truly understand this command, we need to appreciate something fundamental about human nature: the more decent a person is, the more words can wound them. If someone says something even mildly offensive to you once, it bothers you if you’re a decent person. That sensitivity is actually a sign of your decency. When you have iman, even a bad word feels bad. Many of us experience this - someone says something offensive at work, and it ruins your entire day. You lose your appetite, you come home and your family asks what’s wrong, and you don’t even want to talk about it. Words can shut you down mentally. They can hurt more than a physical beating, especially for people of good character.

Nobody was going to be more decent than the Prophet ﷺ. Allah ﷻ protected him in his upbringing in such a way that before he could even be exposed to vile music or speech, Allah ﷻ would put him to sleep. This was even before he became a messenger. The Prophet ﷺ couldn’t stand angry speech or vile language, even coming from believers. When Aisha (RA) once responded to someone who cursed the Prophet ﷺ, he stopped her immediately, even though she was defending him. He didn’t want to hear that kind of language - he couldn’t stand it.

Now imagine: he has to hear the worst kind of language from his people on a daily basis. How could he possibly have the motivation to go back the next day and start with a clean slate, as though he carried no scars from the day before? The answer lies in the night. At night, he talks to Allah ﷻ, and Allah ﷻ cleanses all the damage, rejuvenates him so he can go back again. He has to swim through it all again tomorrow, go right back into the thick of it. Then he comes back battle-scarred, and Allah ﷻ heals him again at night. This happens with him on a nightly basis.

This pattern wasn’t unique to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Consider the beautiful conversation between Allah ﷻ and Musa (AS) in Surah Ta-Ha. “What’s in your hand?” - such a beautiful conversation. Why? Because Musa (AS) gets rejuvenated by that conversation. He carries guilt inside him from long ago, from killing a person. Allah ﷻ removes all those scars, motivates him, brings him close, and then says: now go have the worst conversation of your life. Go talk to the nastiest human being on earth. That most beautiful conversation was preparing him for the nastiest one.

We’ve become too accustomed to willing audiences - people who want to hear what we have to say. The Prophet’s ﷺ majority audience was unwilling. They wanted nothing to do with this message. Yet Allah ﷻ commands: وَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ - be patient over whatever they’re saying. وَٱهْجُرْهُمْ هَجْرًا جَمِيلًا - and abandon them, leave them in the most beautiful way. Don’t leave them angry. Leave them in a decent way. Don’t give them a reason to say “I’m not talking to you, I’m mad at you.” Let them give you all the reasons to be offensive - you don’t give them a reason. Don’t equip them with ammunition to say you left in an insulting way. Leave them beautifully. Speak to them again with a smile, even though they’re insulting you.

Ayah 11

وَذَرْنِى وَٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ أُو۟لِى ٱلنَّعْمَةِ وَمَهِّلْهُمْ قَلِيلًا

And leave Me with those who deny - those of ease - and allow them respite a little.

After commanding patience and beautiful departure, Allah ﷻ shifts the conversation. وَذَرْنِى - leave Me alone. It’s like when your friend gets into a fight and you’re holding him back, and he says “Let me go! Let me go! Let me deal with him myself!” Allah ﷻ is saying to His Prophet ﷺ: don’t worry about them. Let Me deal with them. وَٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ - leave Me and those who lie against you by themselves.

أُو۟لِى ٱلنَّعْمَةِ - the people who are possessors of great luxury. There’s a difference between نِعْمَة and نَعْمَة. Ni’mah is a blessing - it could be guidance, it could be good provision, many things. Na’mah specifically refers to luxury, wealth, the good life, ease and comfort. These deniers living in luxury - وَمَهِّلْهُمْ قَلِيلًا - give them just a little bit of time. Be patient with them just a little bit more. Then you’ll see - I will deal with them Myself.

Ayah 12

إِنَّ لَدَيْنَآ أَنكَالًا وَجَحِيمًا

Indeed, with Us are shackles and burning fire.

إِنَّ لَدَيْنَآ - we have in Our special possession أَنكَالًا - heavy shackles and fetters, وَجَحِيمًا - and a blazing fire.

Ayah 13

وَطَعَامًا ذَا غُصَّةٍ وَعَذَابًا أَلِيمًا

And food that chokes and a painful punishment.

وَطَعَامًا ذَا غُصَّةٍ - food that gets stuck, food that you choke on. الغُصَّة is when something gets lodged in your throat. As the Arabs say, the one who knows best about choking is the one who has choked. This is food meant to be choked on, with thorns coming out of it, so it gets stuck in the throat and doesn’t go down. وَعَذَابًا أَلِيمًا - and on top of that, painful punishment.

Ayah 14

يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَٱلْجِبَالُ وَكَانَتِ ٱلْجِبَالُ كَثِيبًا مَّهِيلًا

On the Day the earth and the mountains will shake, and the mountains will become a heap of sand pouring down.

يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَٱلْجِبَالُ - the Day on which the earth and the mountains are going to shake. وَكَانَتِ ٱلْجِبَالُ كَثِيبًا مَّهِيلًا - and mountains are going to turn into just piles of sand. كَثِيبًا مَّهِيلًا actually means easy to scale, smooth and slippery - just dunes of sand. Mountains will be turned into dunes of sand.

Ayah 15

إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ رَسُولًا شَـٰهِدًا عَلَيْكُمْ كَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ رَسُولًا

Indeed, We have sent to you a messenger as a witness over you, just as We sent to Pharaoh a messenger.

Now the conversation shifts again. After Allah ﷻ spoke to His Prophet ﷺ telling him to be patient, then turned angry at the disbelievers describing their punishment, He now addresses them directly: إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ رَسُولًا - no doubt about it, We were the ones who sent to you especially a messenger, شَـٰهِدًا عَلَيْكُمْ - witnessing over you, كَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ رَسُولًا - just like We sent a messenger to Pharaoh.

Ayah 16

فَعَصَىٰ فِرْعَوْنُ ٱلرَّسُولَ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُ أَخْذًا وَبِيلًا

But Pharaoh disobeyed the messenger, so We seized him with a ruinous seizure.

فَعَصَىٰ فِرْعَوْنُ ٱلرَّسُولَ - then Pharaoh disobeyed the messenger. فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُ أَخْذًا وَبِيلًا - then We grabbed him, We really grabbed him. There’s an emphasis here - أَخْذًا - We grabbed him, and then an adjective on top of that: وَبِيلًا. وَبَال means a great calamity. The grab itself was a calamity. We learned previously that the grab itself wasn’t even the punishment - that was just the beginning. Then Allah ﷻ threw him into the river. The grab itself was crushing, devastating. Pharaoh couldn’t escape.

Ayah 17

فَكَيْفَ تَتَّقُونَ إِن كَفَرْتُمْ يَوْمًا يَجْعَلُ ٱلْوِلْدَٰنَ شِيبًا

Then how can you protect yourselves, if you disbelieve, from a Day that will make the children white-haired?

فَكَيْفَ تَتَّقُونَ - how are you going to protect yourselves? This doesn’t just mean “become a person of taqwa.” تَتَّقُونَ also comes from وِقَايَة - to protect yourself. How are you ever going to escape? How will you protect yourselves إِن كَفَرْتُمْ - if you people disbelieve يَوْمًا - from a Day يَجْعَلُ ٱلْوِلْدَٰنَ شِيبًا - on which the children, the boys, are going to have gray hair? Children will turn gray-haired. Some scholars interpret this to mean the duration and length of that Day. Others say the fear and shock will be so great on that Day that hair will just turn white - the hair color will change because of stress, even for children.

Ayah 18

ٱلسَّمَآءُ مُنفَطِرٌۢ بِهِۦ ۚ كَانَ وَعْدُهُۥ مَفْعُولًا

The heaven will split open thereby; His promise is to be fulfilled.

ٱلسَّمَآءُ مُنفَطِرٌۢ بِهِۦ - the sky itself will be ripping, tearing open as a result of that Day, or because of what’s coming on that Day. كَانَ وَعْدُهُۥ مَفْعُولًا - His promise has already been fulfilled. مَفْعُولًا is the ultimate form, the masdar form of the word فِعْل. This means that as far as Allah ﷻ is concerned, this is already a done deal. It’s not like Allah ﷻ might change His mind about it. It’s done.

Ayah 19

إِنَّ هَـٰذِهِۦ تَذْكِرَةٌ ۖ فَمَن شَآءَ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ سَبِيلًا

Indeed, this is a reminder; so whoever wills may take to his Lord a way.

إِنَّ هَـٰذِهِۦ تَذْكِرَةٌ - no doubt about it, this is a very powerful reminder. فَمَن شَآءَ - and whoever would want to, ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ سَبِيلًا - would then have taken a path towards his Master. This comes from the earlier instruction: ٱتَّخِذْ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ سَبِيلًا - take a path towards your Lord. In the previous passage, we saw the jinn made the free decision through shura, consultation. Now we’re learning that human beings also have this choice: whoever wants should take a path towards their Master.

Ayah 20

۞ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ تَقُومُ أَدْنَىٰ مِن ثُلُثَىِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَنِصْفَهُۥ وَثُلُثَهُۥ وَطَآئِفَةٌ مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مَعَكَ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يُقَدِّرُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ ۚ عَلِمَ أَن لَّن تُحْصُوهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَٱقْرَءُوا۟ مَا تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ ٱلْقُرْءَانِ ۚ عَلِمَ أَن سَيَكُونُ مِنكُم مَّرْضَىٰ ۙ وَءَاخَرُونَ يَضْرِبُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَبْتَغُونَ مِن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ ۙ وَءَاخَرُونَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَٱقْرَءُوا۟ مَا تَيَسَّرَ مِنْهُ ۚ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَقْرِضُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا ۚ وَمَا تُقَدِّمُوا۟ لِأَنفُسِكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْرًا وَأَعْظَمَ أَجْرًا ۚ وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌۢ

Indeed, your Lord knows, [O Muhammad], that you stand [in prayer] almost two thirds of the night or half of it or a third of it, and [so do] a group of those with you. And Allah determines [the extent of] the night and the day. He has known that you [Muslims] will not be able to keep it up and has turned to you in forgiveness, so recite what is easy [for you] of the Quran. He has known that there will be among you those who are ill and others traveling throughout the land seeking [something] of the bounty of Allah and others fighting for the cause of Allah. So recite what is easy from it and establish prayer and give zakah and loan Allah a goodly loan. And whatever good you put forward for yourselves - you will find it with Allah. It is better and greater in reward. And seek forgiveness of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.

This final ayah of Surah Al-Muzzammil is particularly significant. It’s one of the unique ayahs in the Quran that was actually revealed in two parts - one portion in Makkah and one portion in Madinah. The Prophet ﷺ then combined the two portions himself, as the clearest opinion from Abdullah bin Abbas (RA) mentions. The first half is Makkan, the second half is Madinan, and because it’s the same subject matter, Allah ﷻ had it placed together in this surah.

إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ تَقُومُ أَدْنَىٰ مِن ثُلُثَىِ ٱلَّيْلِ - no doubt about it, your Master knows that you are standing right now just a little short of two-thirds of a night, وَنِصْفَهُۥ وَثُلُثَهُۥ - or sometimes half of it, or a third of it. وَطَآئِفَةٌ مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مَعَكَ - and a group from among those that are with you also do the same. Notice Allah ﷻ doesn’t say everybody does it - some people do it. Some people see the benefit of qiyam among the Sahaba (RA), and they’re doing it also.

وَٱللَّهُ يُقَدِّرُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ - and Allah ﷻ is the One Who calculates and dictates the duration, the span of the night and the day. عَلِمَ أَن لَّن تُحْصُوهُ - He already knew that you won’t be able to keep track of it, that you won’t be able to stay on top of it. فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ - so then He accepted your repentance for that matter. Notice the plural has come now. The original instruction was to the Prophet ﷺ - you should be standing, so he’s not excused. But the Companions (RA) tried to do what the Prophet ﷺ does, and they get tired. Allah ﷻ already knew: you’re not going to be able to do that. So He’s already accepted your repentance. Don’t worry about that. That’s why He didn’t reveal it as a mandatory thing for you.

فَٱقْرَءُوا۟ مَا تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ ٱلْقُرْءَانِ - but you should still read whatever is easy for you from the Quran. Whatever is facilitated, whatever is convenient - مَا تَيَسَّرَ. Whatever is easy for you to recite.

My advice to myself and to all of you: you can’t do a third of the night? That might be a little much because right now many of us are on “express lane” mode with our prayers. If you’re not even praying on time, then this conversation isn’t even for you yet - you need to take care of bigger problems first. Fix your prayer times first. If you are praying, then take it step by step.

Nowadays, in certain times of the year, it’s easier because the nights are long. Isha comes early, so go to sleep early. You can get up an hour before Fajr - it’s not that hard. When Fajr is at 5 AM, this is manageable. Yes, there are times in the year when Fajr is at 4:30 AM and Isha is at 10 PM, and then it’s tough. But now it’s easy - Allah ﷻ has made the timing easy for you in some parts of the year. Take advantage of it. Get up and pray even two rak’ahs.

One of the reasons you should be memorizing longer surahs is so you can recite them in qiyam. Quran memorization directly impacts your voluntary prayers. If all you know is Surah Al-Ikhlas and Surah Al-Asr, and that’s every salah - which is fine - but you’re not studying the beautiful meanings, the deep lessons, the tafsir that really moves you. When you memorize surahs with understanding and they truly impact you, your qiyam becomes different.

مَا تَيَسَّرَ - what’s easy for you is not what’s easy for me. We all have different standards, so Allah ﷻ has opened that up.

Now we enter the Madinan portion of the ayah. عَلِمَ أَن سَيَكُونُ مِنكُم مَّرْضَىٰ - Allah ﷻ already knew that some of you are going to be sick. وَءَاخَرُونَ يَضْرِبُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - some of them are going to be traveling in the land. The Hijrah is going to happen to Madinah, military expeditions are going to happen. يَبْتَغُونَ مِن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ - they’re going to be pursuing out of the bounty of Allah ﷻ, meaning even for work. وَءَاخَرُونَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - and others are going to be fighting in Allah’s path.

فَٱقْرَءُوا۟ مَا تَيَسَّرَ مِنْهُ - then just read whatever is easy from it. وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ - and establish the prayer and give zakah. This is crucial: just because you are taking care of the extras, the extras don’t alleviate you from the essentials. That’s unfortunately a mentality some people have developed - that extra deeds can somehow compensate for mandatory deeds. You have people in the Muslim world who are very diligent about tarawih prayers, performing all twenty or however many rak’ahs in the masjid during Ramadan, but they don’t pray five times at any other time in the year. The thinking is that the extra should cover all the other responsibility. But that’s not what the deen teaches. You don’t get to dictate what you get to do, and the extra credit should cover it.

Nothing in the world works like that. You can’t just say “I’m not going to go into work. I owe forty hours of work a week, so I’m just not going to go into the office for a couple of weeks, and then in two weeks I’ll do 120 hours and miss two weeks, and the next two weeks I’ll do 220 hours.” You’re going to get fired. That’s not how anything works. There are standards, there’s discipline, there are times. We already read وَلِلَّهِ الْمَسَاجِدُ - the times of prayer, the places of prostration, the limbs that we have all belong to Allah ﷻ. You don’t get to dictate that yourself.

وَأَقْرِضُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا - and give Allah ﷻ a beautiful loan. Let’s understand this beautiful loan. There’s a difference between when we give a beautiful loan to each other - قَرْضًا حَسَنًا - and giving a beautiful loan to Allah ﷻ.

A typical قَرْض (loan): I lend you $20 and I say, “Okay, pay me back in a month. This is the date you should pay me back.” Done. That’s a loan. But قَرْضًا حَسَنًا is different: I’m giving you $20, and because I’m doing this as a favor to you, I don’t expect it back at a given deadline. “Just give it to me whenever it’s easy for you.” Not setting a deadline is the favor. Not setting a deadline on the return date is what makes it حَسَنًا - beautiful, gracious. That’s what becomes a beautiful loan - an open-ended loan, whenever it’s easy for you to give back.

Now, whatever we do for Allah ﷻ or to Allah ﷻ, Allah ﷻ uses this term, which has profound psychological implications. We give to Allah ﷻ - we give charity, we give time, we give energy to His deen. We cannot expect the returns on that investment, the return on that time, from Allah ﷻ on our terms. Allah ﷻ is saying: let Me decide when I’ll give it back to you. When we give a donation in charity and we remember what the Prophet ﷺ said - مَا نَقَصَ مَالٌ مِنْ صَدَقَةٍ (wealth doesn’t decrease from charity) - this is the promise of Allah ﷻ. He promises: I will give it back.

وَمَا تُقَدِّمُوا۟ لِأَنفُسِكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْرًا وَأَعْظَمَ أَجْرًا - and whatever good you put forward for yourselves, you will find it with Allah ﷻ. It is better and greater in reward.

وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌۢ - and seek forgiveness of Allah ﷻ. Indeed, Allah ﷻ is Forgiving and Merciful.
